quiz_questions.question_text: Seafloor spreading occurs at:

options.text: Subduction zones
Transform boundaries
Continental interiors
Mid-ocean ridges

quiz_questions.explanation: Seafloor spreading occurs at mid-ocean ridges, which are divergent plate boundaries where tectonic plates pull apart and magma rises from the mantle to create new oceanic crust.

quiz_questions.question_text: Who proposed the theory of seafloor spreading?

options.text: J Tuzo Wilson
Charles Lyell
Alfred Wegener
Harry Hess

quiz_questions.explanation: Harry Hess, a Princeton geologist, proposed the theory of seafloor spreading in 1962, explaining how ocean floors expand outward from mid-ocean ridges as new crust forms.

quiz_questions.question_text: Oceanic crust near mid-ocean ridges is _____ than crust far from ridges.

options.text: More eroded
Younger
Thicker
Older

quiz_questions.explanation: Crust is youngest at the ridge where it is freshly formed and gets progressively older with distance from the ridge as it spreads outward over time.

quiz_questions.question_text: Why is all oceanic crust less than about 200 million years old?

options.text: It does not form permanent rocks
Continental crust absorbs it
Older oceanic crust is recycled back into the mantle at subduction zones
It dissolves in seawater over time

quiz_questions.explanation: Oceanic crust is continuously created at mid-ocean ridges and destroyed at subduction zones where it sinks into the mantle, so none survives longer than about 200 million years.

quiz_questions.question_text: The Mid-Atlantic Ridge is an example of a:

options.text: Subduction zone
Transform boundary
Convergent boundary
Divergent boundary

quiz_questions.explanation: The Mid-Atlantic Ridge is a divergent plate boundary running down the center of the Atlantic Ocean, where the North American and Eurasian plates spread apart and new crust forms.

quiz_questions.question_text: Sediment thickness on the ocean floor increases with distance from the ridge because:

options.text: More animals live far from ridges
Volcanoes at ridges prevent sediment from settling
Ocean currents deposit more sediment far away
Older crust has had more time to accumulate sediment

quiz_questions.explanation: Crust farther from ridges is older and has been on the ocean floor longer, giving sediment more time to accumulate on its surface, while new crust near the ridge has virtually no sediment.

quiz_questions.question_text: The rate of seafloor spreading is typically:

options.text: 1 mm per year
2 to 10 cm per year
1 m per year
1 km per year

quiz_questions.explanation: Seafloor spreading occurs at rates of about 2 to 10 centimeters per year, comparable to the rate at which fingernails grow, though this varies between different mid-ocean ridge segments.

quiz_questions.question_text: Sonar mapping of the ocean floor revealed:

options.text: An extensive mountain range (mid-ocean ridge system), the longest on Earth
No notable geological features
Only deep trenches with no mountains
A completely flat surface

quiz_questions.explanation: Sonar mapping in the mid-20th century revealed the mid-ocean ridge system, a continuous underwater mountain chain over 65,000 km long, making it the longest mountain range on Earth.

quiz_questions.question_text: Convection currents in the mantle are thought to:

options.text: Cool Earth's surface
Drive plate movement by circulating hot mantle material
Create fossils in sedimentary rock
Only cause earthquakes without moving plates

quiz_questions.explanation: Convection currents in the mantle, driven by heat from Earth's core, circulate hot material upward at ridges and downward at subduction zones, providing the driving force behind plate tectonics.

quiz_questions.question_text: Iceland sits directly on the Mid-Atlantic Ridge, which is why it has:

options.text: Only earthquakes but no volcanoes
Exclusively glacial activity
Frequent volcanic eruptions and geothermal activity
No geological activity

quiz_questions.explanation: Iceland straddles the Mid-Atlantic Ridge, where the North American and Eurasian plates are pulling apart, causing frequent volcanic eruptions, geysers, and abundant geothermal energy.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is seafloor spreading?

options.text: The erosion of coastal beaches by waves
The deposition of sediment on the continental shelf
The sinking of the ocean floor at subduction zones
The process by which new oceanic crust forms at mid-ocean ridges as tectonic plates move apart and magma rises to fill the gap

quiz_questions.explanation: Seafloor spreading is the process at mid-ocean ridges where tectonic plates diverge, allowing magma from the mantle to rise, cool, and form new oceanic crust that pushes older crust outward.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a mid-ocean ridge?

options.text: A flat area of the ocean floor
A trench at the bottom of the ocean
An underwater mountain chain formed at divergent plate boundaries where new oceanic crust is created
A coral reef formation near the equator

quiz_questions.explanation: A mid-ocean ridge is a continuous underwater mountain system formed at divergent plate boundaries where magma rises from the mantle to create new oceanic crust, such as the Mid-Atlantic Ridge.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is magnetic striping on the ocean floor?

options.text: Symmetric alternating bands of normal and reversed magnetic polarity in ocean floor rocks, recorded as new crust cooled at the ridge
Patterns in ocean currents detected by ships
Magnetic disturbances caused by underwater lightning
Colorful bands of different rock types visible on the seafloor

quiz_questions.explanation: Magnetic striping consists of symmetric bands of normal and reversed magnetic polarity on either side of mid-ocean ridges, formed as iron minerals in cooling lava recorded Earth's periodically reversing magnetic field.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'magnetic reversal' mean?

options.text: A switch in Earth's magnetic field where the north and south magnetic poles trade positions
The movement of magnetic rocks across the ocean floor
The weakening of all magnets on Earth's surface
The Earth spinning in the opposite direction

quiz_questions.explanation: A magnetic reversal is an event in which Earth's magnetic north and south poles swap positions, occurring irregularly over hundreds of thousands to millions of years, and recorded in the rock record.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is paleomagnetism?

options.text: The use of magnets to locate buried treasure
The study of Earth's ancient magnetic field as recorded in rocks, providing evidence of seafloor spreading and plate movement
The magnetic properties of modern volcanic rock only
The strength of Earth's current magnetic field

quiz_questions.explanation: Paleomagnetism is the study of the record of Earth's past magnetic field preserved in rocks, which provided crucial evidence for seafloor spreading through the symmetric magnetic stripe patterns at ridges.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'divergent boundary' mean?

options.text: A boundary where two continents merge together
A boundary where plates collide and one sinks beneath the other
A boundary where plates slide horizontally past each other
A boundary where two tectonic plates move apart from each other, allowing magma to rise and form new crust

quiz_questions.explanation: A divergent boundary is where tectonic plates move away from each other, creating a gap that is filled by rising magma from the mantle, forming new crust and features like mid-ocean ridges.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'subduction' mean?

options.text: The creation of new crust at mid-ocean ridges
The erosion of coastlines by ocean waves
The process by which one tectonic plate descends beneath another into the mantle, recycling oceanic crust
The uplift of mountains by compression

quiz_questions.explanation: Subduction is the process at convergent boundaries where a denser tectonic plate (usually oceanic) slides beneath a less dense plate and descends into the mantle, where it is eventually recycled.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'mantle convection' mean?

options.text: The movement of air in the atmosphere following the established framework
The rotation of Earth's crust, particularly regarding solid in accordance with classical theory
The mixing of ocean water by waves, particularly regarding cooler in most standard conditions
The slow circulation of hot mantle rock driven by heat, with material rising at ridges and sinking at subduction zones

quiz_questions.explanation: Mantle convection is the slow, circular movement of semi-solid rock in the mantle driven by heat from Earth's core, with hot material rising and cooler material sinking, which drives plate tectonics.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is an ocean trench?

options.text: A flat area in the middle of the ocean under normal circumstances
A deep, narrow depression in the ocean floor formed at subduction zones where oceanic crust descends into the mantle
A shallow area near the coast, particularly regarding nearly when evaluated systematically
An underwater volcano at a mid-ocean ridge as typically measured in practice

quiz_questions.explanation: An ocean trench is the deepest type of ocean floor feature, formed at convergent boundaries where one plate subducts beneath another, such as the Mariana Trench which reaches nearly 11,000 meters deep.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'crustal recycling' mean?

options.text: The weathering of continental rock into sand
The movement of sediment along the coast
The continuous process of creating new oceanic crust at ridges and destroying old crust at subduction zones, maintaining a balance
The reuse of building materials from demolished structures

quiz_questions.explanation: Crustal recycling is the ongoing process where new oceanic crust is generated at mid-ocean ridges and old crust is consumed at subduction zones, keeping Earth's surface area and crustal volume relatively constant.

quiz_questions.question_text: A marine geologist examining deep-sea drill cores notices that sediment directly above the basalt ocean floor is 50 million years old at a location 500 km from a mid-ocean ridge, but only 10 million years old at a location 100 km from the ridge. What does this pattern demonstrate?

options.text: The ocean floor gets progressively older with distance from the ridge, confirming seafloor spreading
The drill cores must be contaminated
Sediment is deposited randomly on the ocean floor
Sediment near the ridge is deposited faster

quiz_questions.explanation: The progressive increase in sediment age with distance from the ridge is direct evidence of seafloor spreading: crust closest to the ridge is youngest, and the first sediment to land on it marks its age.

quiz_questions.question_text: A geophysicist towing a magnetometer behind a ship across the Atlantic Ocean records alternating bands of strong and weak magnetic signal. She notices the pattern is symmetrical on both sides of the Mid-Atlantic Ridge. What is the best explanation for this symmetry?

options.text: The magnetometer is producing errors
New crust forms equally on both sides of the ridge and records the same sequence of magnetic reversals, creating mirror-image stripes
Ocean currents create the magnetic patterns
Undersea volcanoes arranged themselves symmetrically by chance

quiz_questions.explanation: As magma cools symmetrically on both sides of a mid-ocean ridge, iron minerals align with Earth's current magnetic field; when the field reverses, new crust records the opposite polarity, creating mirror-image stripes.

quiz_questions.question_text: A research team comparing the age of continental rock (up to 4 billion years old) to oceanic crust (no older than about 200 million years) asks why there is such a dramatic age difference. What is the correct explanation based on seafloor spreading?

options.text: Continental crust was formed first because Earth cooled from the center outward
Continental rock is made of more durable minerals
Oceanic crust dissolves in saltwater after 200 million years
Oceanic crust is continuously recycled through creation at ridges and destruction at subduction zones, while continental crust is too buoyant to subduct

quiz_questions.explanation: Oceanic crust is constantly recycled by subduction, preventing it from surviving more than about 200 million years, while continental crust is too buoyant and thick to subduct, allowing it to persist for billions of years.

quiz_questions.question_text: A documentary crew filming in Iceland stands on a bridge that spans a visible rift valley between the North American and Eurasian plates. They can see the two plates pulling apart. A producer asks what will happen to Iceland over millions of years. What is the most scientifically accurate prediction?

options.text: Iceland will sink beneath the ocean
Iceland will split in half and the two pieces will collide
Iceland will continue to grow wider as new volcanic crust fills the rift created by the spreading plates
Iceland will remain exactly the same size forever

quiz_questions.explanation: As the North American and Eurasian plates continue to spread apart, volcanic activity along the rift in Iceland will produce new crust that fills the growing gap, causing the island to gradually widen over geological time.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student examining a map of the ocean floor notices that heat flow measurements are highest at mid-ocean ridges and lowest far from ridges near continents. She asks her teacher to explain this pattern. What is the correct answer?

options.text: Sunlight heats the water near ridges more than near continents
Volcanic eruptions only occur near continents
Cold ocean currents are concentrated at mid-ocean ridges
Hot mantle material rises directly beneath ridges, producing high heat flow, while crust far from ridges has cooled over time as it moved away

quiz_questions.explanation: High heat flow at mid-ocean ridges is caused by hot mantle material rising to the surface, and as the newly formed crust spreads away from the ridge, it gradually cools, reducing heat flow with distance.

quiz_questions.question_text: A naval commander observes that during World War II, submarine detection equipment revealed an unexpected feature running through the middle of the Atlantic Ocean. After the war, Harry Hess used this data to develop his theory. What feature was discovered, and how did it inspire Hess?

options.text: Flat abyssal plains that disproved continental drift
Underwater river systems flowing between continents
The Mid-Atlantic Ridge, an enormous underwater mountain chain that Hess proposed was a site of new ocean floor creation
Deep ocean trenches, which showed plates were colliding

quiz_questions.explanation: Sonar technology used in World War II revealed the Mid-Atlantic Ridge, and Harry Hess, who had used sonar aboard his transport ship, proposed that this ridge was where new ocean floor was being created.

quiz_questions.question_text: A geochemist analyzes the age of basalt samples collected from various distances along the ocean floor east of the East Pacific Rise. She plots the data and finds a nearly linear relationship between distance from the ridge and rock age. From this, she calculates a spreading rate. If basalt 200 km from the ridge is 4 million years old, what is the spreading rate?

options.text: 4 km per million years, or about 0.4 cm per year
200 km per million years, or about 20 cm per year
800 km per million years, or about 80 cm per year
50 km per million years, or about 5 cm per year

quiz_questions.explanation: Dividing 200 km by 4 million years gives a half-spreading rate of 50 km per million years, which equals about 5 cm per year, consistent with measured spreading rates at the East Pacific Rise.

quiz_questions.question_text: A climate scientist notes that seafloor spreading rates have varied throughout Earth's history. During periods of faster spreading, mid-ocean ridges were larger and displaced more ocean water. She explains this affected sea levels. How would faster spreading rates change global sea levels?

options.text: Sea levels would fluctuate rapidly on a daily basis
Sea levels would drop because more water is absorbed into the mantle
Sea levels would remain unchanged
Sea levels would rise because larger, hotter ridges displace more ocean water, pushing it onto continental margins

quiz_questions.explanation: During periods of fast spreading, mid-ocean ridges are larger and hotter, taking up more volume in ocean basins, which displaces seawater and causes global sea levels to rise and flood continental margins.

quiz_questions.question_text: A deep-sea exploration team using a submersible observes hydrothermal vents (black smokers) along a mid-ocean ridge. They see superheated water shooting out of chimney-like structures, surrounded by unique organisms. A biologist on the team asks what energy source supports life here. What is the correct answer?

options.text: Chemosynthetic bacteria that convert chemicals from the vent water into energy, forming the base of the food chain
Organic matter that falls from the surface
Heat alone sustains the organisms directly
Sunlight that penetrates the deep ocean

quiz_questions.explanation: Hydrothermal vent ecosystems are powered by chemosynthetic bacteria that use hydrogen sulfide and other chemicals from the superheated vent water to produce energy, supporting a unique food web independent of sunlight.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student building a model of seafloor spreading uses two conveyor belts moving apart with modeling clay rising between them to represent new crust. Her teacher asks her to add a feature representing subduction to make the model complete. What should she add, and why?

options.text: A heat lamp to melt the clay in the middle, particularly regarding recycling based on established principles
A downward-curving ramp at the outer edges where the conveyor belt dips beneath another surface, representing oceanic crust being consumed at a trench
A fan blowing on the clay to simulate erosion when considering all relevant factors
Additional clay added at the edges to make the belts thicker as predicted by standard models

quiz_questions.explanation: The model needs subduction at the edges to show that as new crust is created at the ridge, old crust is consumed at the trenches, maintaining Earth's constant surface area through this crustal recycling process.
